conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 10th International Conference on Energy Efficiency and Agricultural Engineering (EE&AE)

title-group.article-title: Innodiversification Approach to Assessing the Sustainability and Effectiveness of Organizations

conf-article-meta.aff:

Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: The article is devoted to the study of the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation based on the innodiversification approach. The authors cluster the correlation coefficients of indicators for the period 2014-2023, identifying linear and nonlinear dependencies. The paper presents mathematical models constructed using linear and nonlinear regression, which make it possible to predict the dynamics of sustainability and efficiency indicators. The results of the study confirm the hypothesis of a significant influence of the time factor on the activities of consumer cooperatives. Quantitative analysis revealed that 54.7 % of all inter-indicator correlations and 61.3 % of indicator-time correlations were high or very high ($\mathbf{R} \geq \mathbf{0. 7}$). The constructed models, with an average approximation error not exceeding 5%, demonstrate high predictive accuracy.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The relevance of the research is due to the need to develop modern approaches to assessing the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation in a dynamically changing economic environment [1]. Consumer cooperation plays an important role in the socio-economic development of regions, and understanding the factors influencing its sustainability is key to making managerial decisions [2]. Existing studies often focus on either financial stability [7], [8] or investment efficiency [5] in isolation. However, an integrated approach that combines these aspects is lacking. The innodiversification approach proposed by the authors, which integrates principles of innovation and diversification, allows for a comprehensive analysis by considering both direct and indirect dependencies between a wide array of indicators [3], [4]. This makes the study particularly significant.

The purpose of the research is to develop and test an innodiversification approach to assessing the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation based on the analysis of correlation dependencies and the construction of mathematical models for predicting the dynamics of key indicators.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Research Methodology
sec.p: The study is based on official annual statistical data for the Novosibirsk Region consumer cooperation societies for the period 2014-2023. The dataset comprises 44 indicators (36 for sustainability, Ni, and 8 for efficiency, Ei) across 10 time periods, resulting in 946 unique correlation pairs. The variables cover key aspects of organizational performance, including fixed assets (Y1-Y3), financial metrics (Y5, Y14, Y16, Y19), human resources (Y6, Y8, Y9), market activity (Y10, Y22-Y30), environmental responsibility (Y31-Y33), and social impact (Y34-Y36). Efficiency indicators (E1-E8) encompass productivity, turnover, and profitability.

All calculations and model constructions were performed using the Python programming language (v 3.11) with the Scikit-learn library for clustering and the Statsmodels library for regression analysis. This environment was chosen for its robustness in statistical modeling and reproducibility.

The paper uses methods of correlation analysis (Pearson's coefficient), data clustering (K-means algorithm for grouping correlation magnitudes), and regression analysis (linear and nonlinear). The authors apply the technology of approximating the function of one variable to construct mathematical models. The "innodiversification approach" is conceptualized as a hierarchical framework for classifying indicators based on the strength and nature of their dependencies. Indicators with a strong direct linear relationship with time (correlation $\mathrm{R} \geq 0.7$) are assigned to the first level of direct influence. Indicators with weaker direct links but strong correlations with other first-level indicators are assigned to a second level of indirect influence, modeled via multivariate regression. This provides a deeper, multilayered understanding of the system's dynamics [7], [8].

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Correlation Analysis
sec.p:
To calculate the correlation coefficients between sustainability (Yi) and efficiency (Ti) indicators for the period 2014-2023, the Pearson correlation coefficient can be used. The Pearson correlation coefficient measures the linear relationship between two variables and takes values from -1 to 1, where [ 5], [6 ]:
•
1 means a perfect positive linear relationship, •
-1 means a perfect negative linear relationship, •
0 means there is no linear relationship.

The analysis of the obtained correlation coefficients suggests grouping them into five clusters (Table 1) [9].
Table I. Clustering of correlation coefficients of consumer cooperation activity indicators for the period 2014-2023
Cluster name Cluster range Number of correlation coefficients in a cluster Cluster share %
Very high correlation 0.90-1.00 296 31,33
High correlation 0.70-0.89 222 23,33
Cluster name 0.50-0.69 145 15,33
Average correlation 0.30-0.49 132 14,00
low correlation −0.30-0.29 151 16,00
Total от -1.0 до 1.0 946 100,00
The correlation coefficients were grouped by absolute values for ease of analysis. To build predictive models, it is important to determine the impact of the time period on sustainability and efficiency. The range of high and very high correlation ($\mathrm{R}=0.7$) included more than half of the correlation coefficients $(54.7 \%)$, which confirms the hypothesis of the existence of a mathematical relationship between the indicators of consumer cooperation. Of particular interest for forecasting is the dependence of sustainability and efficiency indicators on the time period (Table 1) [9].
Table II. Clustering of correlation coefficients of sustainability and efficiency indicators from the time period of the consumer cooperation for 2014-2023
Cluster name Cluster range Number of correlation coefficients in a cluster Cluster share %
Very high correlation 0.90-1.00 15 34,09
High correlation 0.70-0.89 12 27,27
Cluster name 0.50-0.69 8 18,18
Average correlation 0.30-0.49 2 4,55
low correlation 0-0.29 7 15,91
Total от -1.0 до 1.0 44 100,00
The range of high and very high correlation ($\mathrm{R} \geq 0.7$) included 27 correlation coefficients (61.3 %), which confirms the hypothesis that there is a linear dependence of most indicators of sustainability and efficiency on the time period of consumer cooperation.
According to a different diversification approach, these indicators should be attributed to the first level of direct influence of a multilevel mathematical model. As a result, a dynamic matrix was formed, consisting of sustainability and efficiency indicators that have a direct linear dependence on the time period.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Regression Analysis
sec.p:
The results of approximation by a linear function of the dependence of dynamic indicators of consumer cooperation activity on the time period are shown in Table 3.
Table III. Mathematical models of indicators of sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation activities
Indicator names Designation Linear regression
Average annual cost of buildings and structures Y1 $\mathrm{Y} 1=2858,56 * ~\mathrm{T}-5745637,40$
Average annual cost of machinery and equipment Y2 $\mathrm{Y} 2=617,78^{*} ~\mathrm{T}-1243443,47$
Average annual cost of vehicles Y3 $\mathrm{Y} 3=602,81^{*} ~\mathrm{T}-1215033,13$
Average annual amount of funds Y5 Y5=4182,07*T-8425698,67
Average age of staff Y6 Y6=-1,01*T+2089,73
Average salary Y8 Y8=2361,90*T-4745567,73
The average amount of premium payments Y9 Y9=404,88*T-813042,53
Number of shareholders Y10 $\mathrm{Y} 10=-254,78^{*} ~\mathrm{T}+518172,20$
The coefficient of financial independence Y14 $\mathrm{Y} 14=0,02 * ~\mathrm{T}-35,13$
Turnover ratio of accounts payable Y16 $\mathrm{Y} 16=0,33 * ~\mathrm{T}-663,42$
Absolute liquidity ratio Y19 $\mathrm{Y} 19=0,43 * ~\mathrm{T}-864,75$
The level of cooperation of the population Y22 $\mathrm{Y} 22=0,19 * ~\mathrm{T}-382,19$
The share of non-food products in the retail trade turnover of consumer cooperation Y23 $\mathrm{Y} 23=0,67 * ~\mathrm{T}-1323,59$
The number of shops per 1,000 inhabitants of the serviced population Y24 Y24=-0,09*T+180,64
The share of retail trade turnover of consumer cooperation in the total volume of the territory Y25 $\mathrm{Y} 25=-0,09 * ~\mathrm{T}+181,00$
Coverage of monetary income of the population by retail trade turnover of consumer cooperation Y26 $\mathrm{Y} 26=-0,32^{*} ~\mathrm{T}+656,48$
The share of own-made goods in the total retail turnover of consumer cooperation Y28 $\mathrm{Y} 28=-0,28^{*} ~\mathrm{T}+563,01$
Sales volume per shareholder Y29 $\mathrm{Y} 29=6,64 * ~\mathrm{T}-13369,04$
Payment for negative environmental impact, thousand rubles Y30 $\mathrm{Y} 30=-0,06^{*} ~\mathrm{T}+126,13$
The ratio of investments in fixed assets aimed at environmental protection to revenue, % Y31 $\mathrm{Y} 31=1,06 * ~\mathrm{T}-2138,96$
The coefficient of participation of consumer cooperatives in the rural labor market Y33 $\mathrm{Y} 33=-0,10^{*} ~\mathrm{T}+205,01$
The coefficient of participation of consumer cooperatives in achieving the subsistence level Y34 $\mathrm{Y} 34=0,11^{*} ~\mathrm{T}-227,61$
The coefficient of participation of consumer cooperation in the formation of monetary incomes of the rural population Y35 $\mathrm{Y} 35=0,02 * ~\mathrm{T}-38,84$
Indicator names Designation Linear regression
The amount of paid services per capita Y36 $\mathrm{Y} 36=0,05 * ~\mathrm{T}-105,00$
Volume of activity per shareholder E2 $\mathrm{E} 2=6,69 * ~\mathrm{T}-13468,21$
Labor productivity E3 $\mathrm{E} 3=1,98 * ~\mathrm{T}-3987,06$
Turnover ratio E4 $\mathrm{E} 4=0,10 * ~\mathrm{T}-191,71$
Linear regression is one of the most widely used methods for modeling the dependence of variables. In the context of consumer cooperation, linear regression allows us to establish a linear relationship between sustainability and efficiency indicators (Yi and Ei) and the time period (T). The main advantages of linear regression include:
Easy to interpret: Linear models are easy to interpret and allow you to clearly see the impact of time on performance.
Forecasting: the obtained models can be used to predict future values of indicators based on current data.
Significance assessment: linear regression coefficients allow us to assess the significance of each indicator and its contribution to the overall model.
The analysis of indicators of sustainability (Yi) and efficiency (Ei) of consumer co-operation activity shows that linear regression is a reasonable method for modeling the dependence of these indicators on time. The accuracy of the models was estimated by the Average Approximation Error (AAE) parameter, calculated as the mean absolute percentage error between the actual and predicted values. For all linear models presented in Table 3, the AAE did not exceed 5%, which confirms the statistical adequacy and high predictive quality of the selected models. The innodiversification approach makes it possible to justify the choice of each indicator, emphasizing their contribution to the stability and efficiency of consumer cooperation (Table 4) [10].
Table IV. The results of the analysis of mathematical models of indicators of sustainability (ni) and efficiency (ei) of consumer cooperation activities
Naming of the indicator Validity Innodiversification approach
Average annual rate Cost of buildings and structures (Y1) Buildings and structures are an important asset of a consumer cooperative that affects its performance sustainability Investment in infrastructure contributes to diversification and innovation, which increases productivity sustainability of cooperation
Average annual rate cost of machinery and equipment (Y2) Machinery and equipment are key elements of the production process Equipment upgrades and upgrades contribute to improving the company's efficiency and innovation
Average annual cost vehicles (Y3) Vehicles provide logistics and operational cooperation Investment in transport contributes to the diversification of supply chains
Average Annual amount of funds (Y5) Cash provides liquidity and financial stability to the company Cash flow management contributes to the diversification of financial resources
Table IV. The results of the analysis of mathematical models of indicators of sustainability (ni) and efficiency (ei) of consumer cooperation activities
Naming of the indicator Validity Innodiversification approach
Average age of staff (Y6) The age of employees affects the experience and qualifications of employees of the company Diversity age groups promotes innovation and adaptability
Average salary (Y8) Salary the fee affects the motivation and satisfaction of the company's employees Competitive price salaries help attract and retain talented employees
Average amount of bonus payments (Y9) Bonuses encourage employee productivity and loyalty Flexible system bonus payments help diversify the company's motivational strategies
Size shareholders (Y10) Size the number of shareholders affects the scale and sustainability of the cooperation Increase the increase in the number of shareholders contributes to the diversification of sources of financing
Financial Independence Coefficient (Y14) Financial independence ensures the sustainability and autonomy of cooperation Diversification increases the financial sustainability of the company
Ratio turnover of accounts payable (Y16) Turnover rate debt management affects the Bank's liquidity and financial stability Effective debt management contributes to the diversification of financial flows
Ratio absolute liquidity (Y19) Absolute liquidity ensures the cooperative's ability to meet short-term obligations Liquidity management contributes to the diversification of financial resources
Cooperation level population (Y22) Level cooperation affects the scale and sustainability of cooperation Increase the level of cooperation contributes to the diversification of market participants
Fraction non-food products in retail turnover Consumer cooperation (Y23) Diversification increases the sustainability and adaptability of cooperation Diversity it promotes innovation and product diversification
Quantity stores per 1000 inhabitants of the served population (Y24) Density it affects the accessibility and convenience of stores for consumers Increase the number of stores contributes to the diversification of retail outlets
Share of turnover retail trade consumer cooperation in the total volume of the territory (Y25) The share of turnover affects the competitiveness and sustainability of cooperation Increasing the share of turnover contributes to the diversification of the company's market positions
Coverage of monetary incomes of the population by retail trade turnover of consumer cooperation (Y26) Income coverage affects the sustainability and scale of cooperation Increasing income coverage contributes to the diversification of the consumer base
Naming of the indicator Validity Innodiversification approach
Share of own-made goods in total retail trade turnover consumer cooperatives (Y28) Own name production increases the sustainability and competitiveness of cooperation Diversity the company's own products contribute to innovation and product diversification
Sales volume at shareholder (Y29) Sales volume it affects the effectiveness and sustainability of cooperation Increase sales volume contributes to the diversification of the company's revenue
Payment for negative impact on the environment, thousand rub. (Y30) Environmental information responsibility affects the sustainability and reputation of a cooperative Investments in the environment contribute to diversification and innovation
Ratio of fixed capital investment aimed at environmental protection to revenue, % (Y31) Investments in the environment increase the sustainability and reputation of cooperation Environmental investments promote diversification and innovation
Ratio participation of consumer cooperatives in the rural labor market (Y33) Participation in the labor market affects the sustainability and social responsibility of the cooperative Diversity the labor force contributes to the diversification and adaptability of the economy
Ratio participation of consumer cooperatives in achieving the subsistence minimum (Y34) Participation in the achieving the minimum subsistence level affects social sustainability cooperatives Social investment promotes diversification and innovation
Ratio participation of consumer cooperatives in the formation of monetary incomes of rural population (Y35) Participation in income generation affects sustainability and social responsibility cooperatives Diversity sources of income contributes to the diversification and adaptability of the company
Amount of paid services per capita (Y36) Paid services they affect the effectiveness and sustainability of cooperation Diversity it promotes diversification and innovation
Volume of activity per shareholder (E2) The volume of activity affects the efficiency and sustainability of the cooperative Increase the volume of activities contributes to the diversification of the Company's income
Efficiency labor (E3) Efficiency labor market affects the efficiency and sustainability of cooperation Raising productivity contributes to diversification and innovation
Ratio turnover rate (E4) Turnover rate it affects the liquidity and financial stability of the cooperative Effective turnover management contributes to the diversification of financial flows
The results presented in Table IV were obtained through a qualitative analysis based on the innodiversification framework. For each indicator with a established linear relationship to time (from Table III), we provide a dual justification: its intrinsic "Validity" for assessing sustainability/efficiency and its role from the perspective of the "Innodiversification Approach" (i.e., how it contributes to innovation and diversification of the cooperative's activities).
Table 4 presents the results of an analysis using the innodiversification approach of indicators of sustainability (Yi) and effectiveness (Yi) of consumer cooperation activities depending on the time period, including the validity of the choice of each indicator.
According to the innodiversification approach, indicators that are not included in the group of the first level (linear relationship with the time period) can be attributed to the second (indirect) level of influence. Thus, the average annual cost of working capital (Y4) has a correlation coefficient with a time period of only 0.19 and belongs to the cluster "very low correlation", which indicates the absence of linear dependence. However, it has a correlation coefficient of 0.63 (average correlation) with the average annual cost of buildings and structures (Y1) and the probability of their relationship is three times higher than that of the time period. Therefore, it is necessary to check whether there is a connection between them. The test results are shown in table 5 [10].
Table V. The results of the analysis of mathematical models of indicators of sustainability (ni) and efficiency (ei) of consumer cooperation activities
Names of indicators Designatio n Regression
Average annual cost of working capital Y4 Linear: Y4=0,446*Y1+10930,1
Quadratic: $\mathrm{Y} 4=0,00053^{*} \mathrm{Y}^{1}$ 3,08*Y1+60266,4
Cubic: Y4=0,00000000072Y1³+0,000125 Y1 ${ }^{2}$-5,336*Y1+81726,815
Level of education Y7 $\mathrm{Y} 7=-0,1526{ }^{*} \mathrm{Y} 23+17,31$
Volume growth rate ratio activities and salaries Y11 Y11=04843*Y26+0.8044
Volume growth rate ratio operating costs and production costs Y12 $\mathrm{Y} 12=-0,00723^{*} \mathrm{Y} 31+1,033$
Activity growth rate ratio and payments to shareholders Y13 $\mathrm{Y} 13=-0,000041^{*} \mathrm{Y} 10+1,1165$
Accounts receivable turnover ratio Y15 Y15=-0,772*Y6+43,3267
Financial independence ratio Y17 $\mathrm{Y} 17=0,0000071^{*} \mathrm{Y} 4+0,2129$
Turnover ratio of accounts payable Y18 $\mathrm{Y} 18=0,000008^{*} \mathrm{Y} 4+0,15$
Quick liquidity ratio Y20 Y20=1,479*Y19+0,416
Current liquidity ratio Y21 Y21=1,427*Y19+1,316
Sales volume per 1 resident served consumer cooperation organizations Y27 $\mathrm{Y} 27=-0,05737 * \mathrm{Y} 9+2,818$
Names of indicators Designatio n Regression
Share of current environmental protection costs in the organization's expenses, % Y32 $\mathrm{Y} 32=-2,61^{*} \mathrm{Y} 13+1,316$
Fund output E1 $\mathrm{E} 1=-0,0000223^{*} \mathrm{Y} 1+2,93$
Return on assets, % (by net profit) E5 $\mathrm{E} 5=0,00164^{*} \mathrm{Y} 5-10,712$
Return on fixed assets E6 E6=0,00479*Y5-35,442
Current asset turnover ratio E7 $\mathrm{E} 7=-0,000036^{*} \mathrm{Y} 4+2,743$
Return on equity E8 $\mathrm{E} 8=0,001744 * \mathrm{Y} 5+2.93$
The choice of model form (linear, quadratic, cubic) was driven by the data structure and the goal of achieving the best fit. For instance, the relationship for the indicator Y4 (Average annual cost of working capital) was tested against Y1. While a linear model was significant, a quadratic and subsequently a cubic regression were also fitted. The cubic model, of the form Y4 $=a^{*} Y 1^{\wedge} 3+b^{*} Y 1^{\wedge} 2+c^{*} Y 1+d$, was ultimately selected as it provided the lowest AAE, capturing the nonlinear saturation effect where growth in fixed assets (Y1) leads to a disproportionately smaller increase in required working capital (Y4) after a certain point. This reflects a real economic phenomenon of optimizing asset structure.

sec.label: V.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The practical significance of the research lies in the possibility of using the developed models for forecasting and planning the activities of consumer cooperation. The obtained results can be used by the management of cooperatives to increase the stability and efficiency of their work. The theoretical significance lies in the development of a methodology for assessing sustainability and efficiency through the prism of an innodiversification approach, which expands scientific understanding of the factors influencing the activities of cooperatives.

The authors conclude that more than half of the indicators of sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation have a high or very high correlation with the time factor... The authors also emphasize the need for further research of indirect dependencies between indicators using more complex modeling techniques, which can contribute to more accurate forecasting and increase the stability of cooperation. It is recommended to use the developed mathematical models for forecasting and managing key indicators. The authors also emphasize the need for further research of indirect dependencies between indicators, which can contribute to more accurate forecasting and increase the stability of cooperation. The use of an innodiversification approach and mathematical modeling makes the research relevant and practically applicable.

ref.mixed-citation: M. Chernyakov, O. Chistyakova, and V.
